notes: Ipsilon's technology combining IP routing with ATM switching hardware; 5x router performance at fraction of cost; achieved via IFMP (Ipsilon Flow Management Protocol) and GSMP (Generic Switch Management Protocol); lost the standards battle to Cisco's MPLS/Tag Switching; IETF declined to standardize IFMP.

Cabletron's proprietary VLAN and policy-based network management framework; key features: connection-oriented switching, time-of-day/day-of-week access control, call-level accounting; SecureFast VLAN Operational Model published as RFC 2643 (August 1999); technology became obsolete with Cabletron's dissolution.
Protocol for managing ATM switches from IP routing layer; developed by Ipsilon; submitted to IETF; GSMP v3 eventually published as RFC 3292; technology path was superseded by MPLS.
Ipsilon's proprietary flow management protocol; core of IP Switching implementation; submitted to IETF but not adopted as standard; lost to Cisco's Tag Switching (MPLS precursor).

Cabletron's RFC 2124 protocol for policy management and accounting integration across switching platforms; one of several pre-MPLS flow management protocols that did not survive standardization process.

Cisco's competing approach to IP/ATM integration; submitted to IETF; became the basis for MPLS (Multi-Protocol Label Switching, RFC 3031); Tag Switching itself obsolete but MPLS remains fundamental to modern backbone networks.

The technology that ultimately won the IP/ATM integration standards battle; MPLS RFC 3031 published January 2001; derived from Cisco's Tag Switching; remains fundamental to carrier backbone networks, VPNs, and traffic engineering as of 2026.

Cabletron's ASIC-based switching hardware supporting VLAN, broadcast/multicast storm containment, Layer 3 virtual routing; available as modules for MMAC/MMAC-Plus chassis and standalone workgroup units; product line dissolved with Cabletron reorganization.
